segments.caption: The video opens with an overhead view of the Rio 2016 Olympic swimming pool during the Men's 100m Backstroke final, showing swimmers in mid-race. Ryan Murphy (USA, lane 4) is initially behind, with Xu J. (CHN, lane 2) and M. Larkin (AUS, lane 3) leading at the 50m split, while D. Plummer (USA, lane 5) trails. The camera follows the swimmers as they power through the second half, with Murphy making a dramatic surge. The Omega Timer tracks the race, showing splits and the final time of 51.97, which breaks the Olympic Record (OR). Murphy crosses the finish line first, followed closely by Xu J. and D. Plummer. The scene shifts to Murphy celebrating in the pool, then receiving his gold medal at the victory ceremony, standing beside Xu J. (silver) and D. Plummer (bronze). The video concludes with a text overlay highlighting Murphy’s comeback and Plummer’s achievement at 30 in his first Olympics, before transitioning to the next event: Women's 400m IM, shown with a blurred pool view and title card.

segments.caption: The video segment opens with Hungarian swimmer Katinka Hosszú, wearing a white cap with 'HUN' and a red jacket, preparing at the starting block for the Women’s 400m Individual Medley at the Rio 2016 Olympics. She dives into the pool with powerful form, transitioning smoothly into freestyle, breaststroke, backstroke, and butterfly. The camera follows her from above as she dominates the race, maintaining a significant lead over competitors like Maya Dirado (USA) and Emily Overholt (CAN), who trail by over 3 seconds at the 200m mark. The Omega timer shows her split times, and as she approaches the finish, the on-screen display updates to show her breaking the World Record with a time of 4:26.36. After touching the wall, she emerges from the pool, visibly emotional, and is later shown on the podium in a red 'HUNGARY' jacket, holding her gold medal with pride. The victory ceremony features her flanked by silver medalist Maya Dirado (USA) and bronze medalist Mireia Belmonte García (ESP), all smiling under the Olympic rings. Text overlays highlight her achievement: winning gold after three Olympic Games and being at least 4 seconds ahead of the field. The segment transitions to the Women’s 800m Freestyle, with a title card appearing over the pool, signaling the next event.

The on-screen graphic confirms his victory, and Phelps emerges from the water, raising both arms in triumph, celebrating his win with a joyful expression. The Rio 2016 branding and Olympic rings are visible throughout, and the pool’s vibrant blue water and colorful lane dividers add to the dynamic atmosphere. segments.caption: The video captures a high-intensity Men's 200m Butterfly race at the Rio 2016 Olympics, beginning with an underwater view of swimmers diving into the pool, their streamlined bodies cutting through the blue water. The camera quickly transitions to an overhead shot, showing the swimmers in their respective lanes, with lane dividers in vibrant colors (red, yellow, blue, green) marking the course. The Omega Timer displays the race progress, starting at 2.6 seconds and advancing rapidly. Swimmers, including Michael Phelps in lane 4 (black cap with 'PHELPS' and American flag), T. Pereira (Brazil, lane 3, green cap), and R. Lochte (USA, lane 5, black cap), are seen executing powerful butterfly strokes, creating splashes and bubbles. At the 50m mark, the on-screen graphics show Phelps with a split of 24.74, just 0.15 seconds behind the World Record, while Pereira and Lochte trail by +0.17 and +0.29 seconds respectively. The camera alternates between overhead and underwater views, capturing the intense competition and synchronized movements. By the 100m mark, the timer reads 53.44, with Lochte now leading slightly, followed closely by Pereira and Phelps, all within 0.01 seconds of each other. The swimmers continue with strong, rhythmic strokes, their arms slicing through the water as they approach the final turn. The Rio 2016 branding is visible on the pool walls, and the crowd’s energy is palpable, though not directly shown. The race remains tightly contested, with no clear leader emerging, setting the stage for a dramatic finish.

segments.caption: The video captures the intense final stretch of the Men's 100m Butterfly at the Rio 2016 Olympics, with swimmers executing powerful butterfly strokes in the Olympic-sized pool marked by colorful lane dividers. The camera follows the pack as they approach the finish, with the Omega timer showing times approaching 50 seconds. At the 50m mark, the on-screen graphics display 'WR 49.82' and 'OR 50.58', and the race concludes with a new Olympic Record of 50.39 set by Joseph Schooling of Singapore, who is shown in a close-up celebrating in the water, removing his goggles, and smiling triumphantly. The crowd erupts in cheers, with fans waving Singaporean flags and celebrating the historic win. The video then transitions to the podium ceremony, where Joseph Schooling stands in the center wearing a green and yellow Singaporean tracksuit, flanked by silver medalist Chad Guy Bertrand LE CLOS of South Africa in a green and yellow tracksuit, and bronze medalist Michael Phelps of the USA in a navy USA jacket with red sleeves. The trio raises their hands in unison, celebrating the moment under the Rio 2016 branding. The atmosphere is electric, with bright stadium lighting and a packed audience adding to the grandeur of the Olympic moment.

segments.caption: The video segment opens with a celebratory scene from the Rio 2016 Olympics, showing fans in the stands waving flags and cheering, including a prominent American flag and a Hungarian flag. The focus then shifts to the podium ceremony for the Men's 100m Butterfly, where four swimmers stand on the podium: Joseph Schooling (Singapore) in a red and white tracksuit, Michael Phelps (USA) in a navy and red USA jacket, Chad Guy Bertrand Le Clos (South Africa) in a green and yellow tracksuit, and Laszlo Cseh (Hungary) in a red 'HUNGARY' jacket. All four athletes are seen holding their medals, with Schooling proudly displaying his gold, while Phelps, Le Clos, and Cseh share a silver medal in a historic three-way tie. The athletes interact with each other, smiling and posing for the cameras, with Schooling raising his arms in triumph. On-screen graphics confirm the event as 'Men's 100m Butterfly Victory Ceremony' and list the medalists. segments.caption: The video captures the final stretch of the Men's 4 x 100m Medley Relay at the Rio 2016 Olympics, focusing on the intense backstroke leg. The camera alternates between underwater and overhead views, showcasing swimmers in powerful, synchronized strokes. The United States team, led by Ryan Murphy in lane 4, is seen making a dramatic comeback, initially trailing but surging ahead in the final 50 meters. The Omega timer displays splits and the race clock, with the USA closing the gap on Great Britain and China. As the swimmers approach the 350m mark, the USA takes the lead, and at the 400m finish, they cross the line first with a time of 3:27.95, setting a new Olympic Record. The on-screen graphics confirm the USA's victory, and the swimmers celebrate in the pool, with Ryan Murphy raising his arms in triumph, visibly elated. The Rio 2016 branding is visible on the pool walls, and the crowd's excitement is palpable. The final shot shows the USA team celebrating their gold medal win, with the 'Winner - Men's 4 x 100m Medley Relay' graphic displayed prominently.

segments.caption: The video segment opens with a jubilant scene of Michael Phelps and his teammates celebrating their victory in the Men's 4 x 100m Medley Relay at the Rio 2016 Olympics. Phelps, shirtless and beaming, is seen laughing and embracing his teammates, including Ryan Murphy, who is also shirtless and smiling broadly. The team, clad in blue and red USA swimwear, walks along the pool deck, waving to the cheering crowd in the packed arena. The camera follows Phelps as he raises his arms triumphantly, acknowledging the fans. The scene transitions to the victory ceremony, where the U.S. team stands on the podium in matching navy and red USA jackets, proudly holding their gold medals. The on-screen graphic confirms the results: United States of America (Gold), Great Britain (Silver), Australia (Bronze). The video then cuts to a blue wooden-textured screen with white text stating, 'Phelps' career ended with an Olympic record (If he doesn't come back),' followed by a list of 'Crazy splits' detailing each swimmer's leg: Ryan Murphy's 51.85 (WR), Adam Peaty's 56.59, Kyle Chalmers' 46.72, and Nathan Adrian's 46.74. The segment concludes with a celebratory animation of a swimmer emerging from the water amidst confetti, with the text 'CONGRATS TO THE SWIMMERS!!' followed by a hand-drawn 'THANKS FOR WATCHING' and a final call-to-action screen with 'PLEASE LIKE & SUBSCRIBE' in bold red and black text against a snowy backdrop.
